← all tutorials

Consistent Hashing (Load Balancing) — interactive 📖 CONSISTENT_HASHING_LB.md 📄 consistent_hashing_lb.py

📖 Full guide (.md, green) — the long-form written explanation.  📄 Source code (.py, orange) — the ground-truth logic this page is verified against.
Keys and nodes are both hashed onto a ring [0, 2³²). A key belongs to the first node clockwise. Add a node and only its arc moves (~K/N keys), not ~80% of them like naive hash % N. Everything below is recomputed in JS with the identical MD5-based hash as consistent_hashing_lb.py  |  🔗 replication lives on these rings →

① The carnage comparison — add one node, count the keys that must move

What to do: drag N (node count). Going N→N+1 nodes, mod-hash (hash(key)%N) remaps ~N/(N+1) of all keys (a thundering herd of cache misses); consistent hashing remaps only ~1/(N+1) (the new node's arc).
same MD5 hash as consistent_hashing_lb.py Section A

② The ring — nodes on a circle; each key points clockwise to its owner

What to do: raise vnodes per node and watch the colored key-band even out (load balancing). Hit add E / remove B and watch only the changed arc's keys jump (red = remapped).
keys drawn as dots on the band; owners by color
Thick dots = physical nodes (labeled). Thin ticks = virtual-node positions. A key's owner = first node met walking clockwise from its hash (wrap past 2³² → 0).

③ Virtual nodes cure the load skew — V=1 vs V=150

What to do: drag vnodes. At V=1 the busiest node holds many× the quietest (one random arc each); by V=150 load balances to within ~10%. The gold line marks the ideal K/N per node.
nodes A B C D · matches consistent_hashing_lb.py Section E
check: … JS ring + MD5 reproduce consistent_hashing_lb.py exactly: adding E to {A,B,C,D} @ V=150 remaps … keys (py: 179); V=3 sample owners match py Section B; V=150 load is balanced.
